content:
# John Chapter 9 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in John Chapter 9 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 9:2 — "who sinned, this man or his parents" vs. "why was this man born blind? Was it because of his own sins or his parents' sins?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Rabbi, who sinned, this [one] or the parents of him, that he was born blind?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Rabbi, who sinned, this man or his parents, that he was born blind?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "'Rabbi,' his disciples asked him, 'why was this man born blind? Was it because of his own sins or his parents' sins?'" | Dynamic; splits into two questions; adds "because of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek is a single question with a binary option. PNEUMA splits it and adds causal language ("because of") not in the original.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:3 — "that the works of God might be displayed in him" vs. "so the power of God could be displayed in healing him"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "but [it was] that the works of the God might be revealed in him" | Literal; "revealed" |
| LOGOS | "but that the works of God might be displayed in him" | Formal; "displayed" |
| PNEUMA | "This happened so the power of God could be displayed in healing him." | Dynamic; "power" instead of "works"; adds "in healing him" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA changes "works" (ἔργα) to "power" and adds "in healing him" — narrowing the scope. The "works of God" in John encompasses more than just the healing.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:4 — "We must work" vs. "I must work" — textual variant

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "We must work the works of the [one] having sent me" | Follows Sinaiticus ἡμᾶς ("we") |
| LOGOS | "We must work the works of him who sent me" | Same Sinaiticus reading |
| PNEUMA | "We must quickly carry out the tasks assigned us by the one who sent me." | Sinaiticus "we"; adds "quickly" and "tasks assigned" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. All three correctly follow the Sinaiticus reading "we" (not "I"). PNEUMA adds "quickly" and paraphrases "works" as "tasks assigned us."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Consensus on textual variant.

---

## 9:6 — "anointed the man's eyes" vs. "spread the mud over the blind man's eyes" vs. "smeared the mud upon the eyes"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he smeared the mud upon the eyes of him" | Literal; "smeared" for ἐπέχρισεν |
| LOGOS | "he anointed the man's eyes with the mud" | Formal; "anointed" for ἐπιχρίω |
| PNEUMA | "spread the mud over the blind man's eyes" | Dynamic; "spread" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. ἐπιχρίω can mean "anoint/smear/apply." "Anointed" (LOGOS) has a deliberate sacred connotation that connects to the Χριστός (Christ = "anointed one") theme. EMET's "smeared" is cruder; PNEUMA's "spread" is neutral.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:9 — "I am the man" vs. "I am [he]" vs. "Yes, I am the same one!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "That [one] said, 'I am [he].'" | Literal; ἐγώ εἰμι — notes possible echo |
| LOGOS | "He kept saying, 'I am the man.'" | Formal; adds "the man" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "the beggar kept saying, 'Yes, I am the same one!'" | Dynamic; "Yes"; "the same one"; exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. The Greek ἐγώ εἰμι here functions as simple self-identification ("I am [he]"), though the repeated formula may carry a faint echo of Jesus' ἐγώ εἰμι statements. "I am the man" makes the reference clear.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:10 — "how were your eyes opened?" vs. "Who healed you? What happened?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "How then were the eyes of you opened?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Then how were your eyes opened?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "Who healed you? What happened?" | Dynamic; replaces with two different questions |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA replaces the actual Greek question ("How were your eyes opened?") with two different questions not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:16 — "How can a man who is a sinner do such signs?" vs. "how could an ordinary sinner do such miraculous signs?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "How is a sinful man able to do such signs?" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "How can a man who is a sinner do such signs?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "But how could an ordinary sinner do such miraculous signs?" | Dynamic; adds "ordinary" and "miraculous" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds both "ordinary" and "miraculous" — neither word is in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:22 — "confess Jesus to be Christ" vs. "saying Jesus was the Messiah"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "if anyone confessed him [as] Christos, he would be put out of the synagogue" | Literal; "Christos" |
| LOGOS | "if anyone should confess Jesus to be Christ, he was to be put out of the synagogue" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "anyone saying Jesus was the Messiah would be expelled from the synagogue" | Dynamic; "Messiah" instead of "Christ"; "expelled" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Confess...to be Christ" preserves the formal confession language (ὁμολογέω). PNEUMA's "saying" is weaker than "confess."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:24 — "Give glory to God" — rendering

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Give glory to the God!" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "Give glory to God." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "God should get the glory for this, not Jesus! We know this man Jesus is a sinner." | Dynamic; interprets the formula; adds "not Jesus" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Give glory to God" is a judicial oath formula (cf. Joshua 7:19), not an instruction to praise God instead of Jesus. PNEUMA's interpretation ("not Jesus") imports a meaning not in the text.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:25 — "though I was blind, now I see" vs. "I was blind, and now I can see!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "One thing I know: that being blind, now I see." | Literal |
| LOGOS | "One thing I do know, that though I was blind, now I see." | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "But I know this: I was blind, and now I can see!" | Dynamic; "can see" adds ability; exclamation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. This is one of the most iconic verses in the NT. "Though I was blind, now I see" has entered the English language (cf. "Amazing Grace"). PNEUMA's "can see" weakens the declarative force.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:30 — "Why, this is an amazing thing!" vs. "How remarkable!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For in this is the marvel" | Literal; "marvel" for θαυμαστόν |
| LOGOS | "Why, this is an amazing thing!" | Formal; captures the man's incredulity |
| PNEUMA | "'How remarkable!' the man replied." | Dynamic; "remarkable" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. θαυμαστόν means "marvelous/amazing/astonishing." LOGOS captures the man's sarcastic incredulity well. Minor difference from PNEUMA.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. Minor divergence.

---

## 9:34 — "You were born in utter sin" vs. "You were born a total sinner!"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In sins you were born entirely, and you teach us?" | Literal; Greek word order |
| LOGOS | "You were born in utter sin, and would you teach us?" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "'You were born a total sinner!' they answered. 'Are you trying to teach us?'" | Dynamic; "total sinner"; splits |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. "Born in utter sin" is more precise than "total sinner" — the Greek says "in sins" (ἐν ἁμαρτίαις), not that he IS sin.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:39 — "that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "that the [ones] not seeing may see, and the [ones] seeing may become blind" | Literal |
| LOGOS | "that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind" | Formal |
| PNEUMA | "to give sight to the blind and to show those who think they see that they are blind" | Dynamic; "think they see" — interpretive |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds "think they" which is interpretive — the Greek says "those who see" (οἱ βλέποντες), not "those who think they see." The irony is that they DO see physically but not spiritually.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:41 — "your guilt remains" vs. "you remain guilty"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the sin of you remains" | Literal; "sin" for ἁμαρτία |
| LOGOS | "your guilt remains" | Formal; "guilt" |
| PNEUMA | "you remain guilty because you claim you can see" | Dynamic; adds "because you claim you can see" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Followed LOGOS. PNEUMA adds an explanatory clause ("because you claim you can see") that repeats the preceding information. The Greek is more terse and powerful in its brevity.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

# Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Count | Typical Resolution |
|---|---|---|
| PNEUMA interpretive expansion (adding words) | 9 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| PNEUMA dynamic paraphrase (restructuring) | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| EMET literal structure | 5 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Word choice divergence | 4 | Resolved in favor of LOGOS |
| Textual variant (ἡμᾶς v.4) | 1 | Consensus (all follow Sinaiticus) |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Chapter 9 is one of the most narratively compelling chapters in John, and the divergences are primarily stylistic rather than theological. LOGOS and SOPHIA show near-perfect alignment throughout, with LOGOS and SOPHIA consensus texts virtually identical. The main divergences come from PNEUMA's additions: interpretive glosses ("ordinary sinner," "miraculous signs," "think they see"), explanatory expansions ("not Jesus," "because you claim you can see"), and narrative embellishments that add words to the text. EMET's distinctive contribution is preserving the Greek participle structures and word order, which in this narrative chapter occasionally has rhetorical force (e.g., v.34's front-positioned "In sins you were born entirely").

The chapter demonstrates that when John writes narrative (rather than discourse), all three translators converge more closely, since narrative requires less interpretive choice than theological argumentation.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
